ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chilli Paneer; closest parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03302114) as paneer is a fresh unaged cottage-style cheese.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Chilli Paneer entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chilli Paneer or paneer-based prepared dishes.

allergens: gluten (soy sauce, cornflour batter may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chilli Paneer is approximately 250 g, comprising battered and fried paneer cubes tossed with peppers, onions, and Indo-Chinese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese) and Chilli Paneer dish — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sauce and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides paneer per-100g values (energy 265 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 20.8 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g per 100 g raw paneer). Chilli Paneer as a prepared dish includes batter coating, frying oil absorption, and sauce components (soy sauce, ketchup, cornflour). Nutrient values for the finished dish are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 50% paneer, 15% batter (cornflour/wheat flour), 10% oil absorbed during frying, 25% vegetables and sauce. Resulting per-100g values for the prepared dish are derived from this composition model. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for capsicum (NDB 11333), onion (NDB 11282), and soy sauce (NDB 16124) components.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian dishes and paneer-based preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried cheese dishes and mixed vegetable stir-fry with sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chilli Paneer exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on: (1) paneer itself has a very low GI (~15–27) as a high-protein, high-fat dairy food; (2) the batter coating and cornflour contribute moderate-GI carbohydrates (GI ~65 for cornflour); (3) the sauce components (ketchup, soy sauce) contribute small amounts of sugar; (4) the overall dish GI is moderated by the dominant fat and protein content of paneer. Weighted estimate yields GI ~45. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 33.75) / 100 = 15.2 (serving size 250 g, carbohydrate 13.5 g/100g × 2.5 = 33.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100g; ~1200 mg per serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Chilli Paneer to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~15 per serving) from batter coating and sauce sugars, partially offset by high protein and fat content of paneer
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Chilli Paneer in moderate portions, as the high protein and fat content of paneer attenuates the glycemic response, but the batter and sauce contribute meaningful carbohydrates (~34 g per serving) that require carbohydrate counting and insulin adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.1 g/100g; ~15 g per serving) from paneer and frying oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Chilli Paneer due to its saturated fat and sodium content; choosing the dry version over gravy, requesting less oil, and limiting portion size to 150 g can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Paneer is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, India, and the Indian diaspora. It consists of cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are coated in a seasoned cornflour and flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then stir-fried with sliced capsicum (bell pepper), onion, green chillies, garlic, and ginger in a bold sauce made from soy sauce, tomato ketchup, chilli sauce, and vinegar. The dish can be served dry (as a starter or snack) or with a thicker gravy (as a main course accompaniment to rice or naan). The texture contrasts crispy fried paneer exterior with a soft, milky interior, and the sauce delivers a balance of spicy, tangy, and umami flavours. Nutritionally, Chilli Paneer is energy-dense (~218 kcal/100g) with significant protein (~9.8 g/100g) and fat (~14.2 g/100g) from paneer, and moderate carbohydrates (~13.5 g/100g) from the batter and sauce. It is a good source of calcium (~210 mg/100g) and vitamin C (~18 mg/100g) from the peppers. The dish is high in sodium (~480 mg/100g) due to soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), moderated by the high protein and fat content of paneer. It is vegetarian and suitable for lacto-vegetarians but not vegans due to the dairy content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chilli Paneer?
Is Chilli Paneer high in sodium?
What is the difference between dry and gravy Chilli Paneer?
Is Chilli Paneer an Indian or Chinese dish?
What are the main ingredients in Chilli Paneer?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN provides direct paneer nutrient data as the primary ingredient; however, Chilli Paneer as a prepared dish required weighted-ingredient composition modelling incorporating batter, frying oil absorption, and sauce components. No single approved source provides a direct per-100g analysis of the finished Chilli Paneer dish. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are derived from a weighted-ingredient composition model: approximately 50% paneer (ICMR-NIN), 15% batter (cornflour/wheat flour, USDA SR Legacy), 10% absorbed frying oil (USDA SR Legacy), and 25% vegetables and sauce (USDA SR Legacy). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for paneer and is not a significant contributor in this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for paneer or the sauce components. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100g from frying oil; this is a trace estimate. The restaurant serving this dish (Jewel Of India, Boat Quay, Singapore) is an Indian vegetarian restaurant, confirming the vegetarian classification. Price SGD 15.90 per serving preserved from input. The food_name has been cleaned from the emoji-decorated input '❣️Chilli Paneer❣️' to the canonical English form 'Chilli Paneer'. Cuisine tag from input was the restaurant name; corrected to 'Indian' as the cuisine type. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore in an Indian restaurant context.

improvement_actions_taken: Cleaned food_name from emoji-decorated input to canonical 'Chilli Paneer'.
Corrected cuisine field from restaurant name to 'Indian'.
Applied Singaporean dish multilingual rules: added Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names.
Built weighted-ingredient composition model for per-100g nutrients using ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy.
Documented -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg with explicit notes.
Estimated GI at 45 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate with full documentation.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
